thinking-before-changing-data.label: Thinking before changing data
thinking-before-changing-data.summary: Learn why previewing and filtering are essential before updating or deleting data in SQL. Build safe habits to avoid accidental data loss and ensure only intended records are changed.

sketch2.title: Accidental Data Loss Is Hard to Undo

multi-why-preview-important.title: Why is previewing with SELECT important before UPDATE or DELETE?
multi-why-preview-important.prompt: Select all reasons why you should preview rows with a SELECT statement before running an UPDATE or DELETE.
multi-why-preview-important.hint: Think about what could go wrong if you skip the preview.

help.concept: Previewing helps you avoid mistakes and ensures only intended rows are changed or deleted.
help.hint_1: Previewing shows which rows match your filter and helps prevent accidental changes.
help.hint_2: It helps you catch mistakes before they affect your data.

fillblank-where-required.title: The role of WHERE in data changes
fillblank-where-required.prompt: Choose the best value for the first missing blank in the statement.
fillblank-where-required.hint: It's the clause that filters which rows are affected.

help.concept: The the missing term clause limits the rows affected by UPDATE or DELETE.
help.hint_1: It's the same clause used to filter rows in SELECT.
help.hint_2: Without this clause, all rows would be changed.

sketch-preview-before-change.title: Preview Before You Change
sketch-preview-before-change.bodyMarkdown:
Before running an `UPDATE` or `DELETE` statement, always preview the rows you intend to affect. Use a `SELECT` query with the same `WHERE` clause to see which records will be changed. For example:

```sql
-- Preview rows to be updated
SELECT * FROM inventory_items WHERE status = 'draft';
```

This helps you confirm that your filter is correct and prevents accidental changes to unintended rows.

sketch-why-where-important.title: Why WHERE Is Critical
sketch-why-where-important.bodyMarkdown:
Running `UPDATE` or `DELETE` without a `WHERE` clause will affect **all rows** in the table. For example:

```sql
DELETE FROM inventory_items;
```

This statement deletes every row! Always use a `WHERE` clause to target only the rows you want to change:

```sql
DELETE FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1;
```

This deletes only test items, keeping your real data safe.

sketch-accidental-data-loss.title: Accidental Data Loss Is Hard to Undo
sketch-accidental-data-loss.bodyMarkdown: Unlike a bad `SELECT` query, which only displays data, a mistaken `UPDATE` or `DELETE` can permanently change or remove records. Restoring lost data is often difficult or impossible without a backup. That's why it's crucial to double-check your filters and preview the affected rows before making changes.
